item.text:
I found the bug in [demo/rate_limiter.py](/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-uglee-Coding-ralphton-research/16c236c5-e508-4436-9d28-e8588d7300ac/scratchpad/omb-clean-test/demo/rate_limiter.py:13). The fix is to assign the pruned timestamp list back to `self.calls` before checking the limit:

```python
def allow(self) -> bool:
    now = time.time()
    self.calls = [t for t in self.calls if now - t < self.window]
    if len(self.calls) < self.limit:
        self.calls.append(now)
        return True
    return False
```

I could not apply the patch because this session’s filesystem is read-only and approvals are disabled. I did verify the behavior: the current code keeps expired timestamps like `[0.0, 1.0, 11.5]`; the fixed algorithm prunes them to `[11.5]`.

--- Learning check ---

Why does assigning the filtered list back to `self.calls` fix the memory growth without changing the rate limiter’s intended allow/deny behavior?
